title: Tech Support Scams: Fake Virus Alerts & Remote Access Fraud
meta_description: "Your computer has a virus." Tech support scammers use pop-ups and calls to steal money and access. Learn how to spot and stop these scams.
overview: Tech support scams are fraudulent schemes where scammers pose as legitimate technical support personnel, often claiming to be from well-known companies like Microsoft or Apple. They convince victims that their computers have critical issues or malware infections and demand money for services they don't need. According to the Federal Trade Commission (FTC), tech support scams cost consumers over $105 million in 2021 alone. These scams are particularly dangerous because they can lead not only to financial loss but also to significant data breaches, putting sensitive personal information at risk.
how_it_works: Step 1: Scammers contact the victim through a pop-up ad or unsolicited phone call, claiming there's an urgent problem with their computer.
Step 2: They ask for remote access to the victim’s device and may install software that allows them full control over the system.
Step 3: The scammer then identifies non-existent problems, such as malware or system errors, which need immediate repair.
Step 4: To fix these fake issues, they demand payment, often via gift cards, wire transfers, or direct bank payments.
examples: John received a phone call from someone claiming to be from Microsoft technical support. They said his computer had viruses and needed immediate cleaning for $300. John paid over the phone only to find out later that his device was perfectly fine.

Maria got an email with a link to what looked like her antivirus software indicating she had critical security issues. After clicking, a call center operator took control of her system and demanded $500 to fix non-existent problems.
red_flags: Unexpected contact from tech support via phone or pop-up ads
Demands for remote access to your computer to 'fix' issues
Requests for payment in unusual forms like gift cards
Pressure to act quickly before a 'serious problem' worsens
Claims that you have won a prize but need to pay fees first
what_to_do: Hang up and independently verify the company’s contact information if contacted by phone.
Never grant remote access to your device from unknown sources.
Install legitimate security software from known, trusted providers.
Report any suspicious activity to the FTC at IdentityTheft.gov.
entry_points: Pop-up ads on websites that claim your computer is infected
Unsolicited phone calls posing as tech support
Emails with links to fake security software

immediate.title: First 24 Hours
steps: Disconnect your computer from the internet and power off to prevent further unauthorized access.
Contact your bank or credit card company immediately if you provided financial information. Request a block on any transactions made by the scammer and consider closing compromised accounts.
Change passwords for all online accounts, especially those related to banking, email, and social media, using strong, unique passwords.
Run a full antivirus scan on your computer using trusted software such as Malwarebytes or Norton. If you don't have one installed, download it from the official website.

reporting.title: File Reports
steps: Report to FTC at reportfraud.ftc.gov providing details of the scam including dates and any amounts involved.
File a complaint with FBI IC3 at ic3.gov, giving them all pertinent information about your encounter.
Contact your local police department for a case number, which can be helpful if you need to provide proof of fraud later on.
File a report with your state Attorney General's office, as they often have consumer protection units that deal specifically with such scams.

financial.title: Financial Recovery
steps: If you paid via gift cards, contact the company to see if there are any steps for reimbursement or reporting fraudulent use.
Freeze your credit at all three bureaus (Equifax: equifax.com/personal/credit-report-services, Experian: experian.com/freeze, TransUnion: transunion.com/credit-freeze) to prevent identity theft.
Set up fraud alerts with each of the credit reporting agencies and request a free credit report from annualcreditreport.com to check for any unauthorized activity.

identity.title: Protect Your Identity
steps: If you shared personal information like your Social Security number or date of birth, monitor your credit reports and consider enrolling in an identity theft protection service.
Consider filing a report with the IRS to prevent tax-related identity theft if sensitive financial details were compromised.

emotional.title: It's Not Your Fault
steps: Remember that tech support scams are designed to intimidate and confuse you, making it easier for scammers to exploit your fears.
Reach out to a trusted friend or family member to talk about what happened. Sharing your experience can help alleviate feelings of shame or embarrassment.
Contact the AARP Fraud Watch Network helpline at 877-908-3360 for free support and resources.

followup.title: Follow-Up (Weeks 2-8)
steps: Monitor your credit reports weekly at annualcreditreport.com to spot any unauthorized activity early.
Be vigilant for follow-up scams that might target previous victims, and report any suspicious contacts to the FTC or IC3.
Consider using a password manager to create strong passwords for all of your accounts, and enable two-factor authentication wherever possible.
